narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to flag concerns for admission. Your profile shows strong aspects, like coming from an upper-middle-income family and planning to study full-time, but it also highlights challenges: your Year 1 school decile of 2 (well below the average of about 5.7, meaning you ranked in the lower 20% of peers), a similar Year 3 decile of 2 (versus an average near 5.5), an LSAT score of 27.5 (compared to the typical 36.7), and an undergraduate GPA of 2.8 (below the average 3.21). As a Black female applicant, you're in underrepresented groups—Black students make up just 4.6% of the dataset, and females 43.6%—but these didn't sway the outcome much here. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other factor since it was so much lower than typical. Next in influence came your LSAT score, also low relative to peers, nudging the odds further toward not passing. Your Year 1 decile ranked third in impact, again contributing to the risk because of its below-average standing. Family income came fourth, with your upper-middle bracket having a slight pull toward failure in this context, followed by race at fifth—your background as Black had a modest influence in that direction. Your undergraduate GPA was sixth, its lower value adding to the concern, while full-time status and gender had the least sway, barely tipping the scales toward risk. Overall, your academic markers like deciles, LSAT, and GPA stood out as weaker than most applicants', stacking up against a dataset where stronger early and later grades, higher test scores, and GPAs typically predict bar success. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 36 if possible, as it's a top influencer and improvable with targeted prep; build study habits now to lift future grade deciles if you're still in school; and invest heavily in bar exam prep courses tailored to your profile, since low early academics signal a need for extra support. Many students in similar spots turn things around by addressing these gaps head-on—you have the determination, and small wins here could shift your predicted odds dramatically. Keep pushing; reapplying with improvements often leads to success.
